Step 1: Establish the diagnosis  
– A 17-year-old with normal BMI, recurrent binge-eating followed by self-induced vomiting, enamel erosion, parotid enlargement → bulimia nervosa.  

Step 2: Determine the metabolic consequence of repeated vomiting  
– Gastric fluid is rich in HCl.  Chronic vomiting causes loss of H+ and Cl– → extracellular volume contraction → “contraction alkalosis.”  

Step 3: Predict serum electrolyte changes  
– Loss of H+ shifts the equilibrium toward ↑HCO3– → metabolic alkalosis (↑serum bicarbonate).  
– Loss of Cl– in vomitus → hypochloremia (↓serum chloride).  
– (Also note: K+ depletion from vomiting and secondary hyperaldosteronism, but the key paired finding here is Cl– and HCO3–.)

Step 4: Match to the answer choices  
– The combination “↓ (first value), ↑ (second value)” corresponds to ↓Cl– and ↑HCO3–.

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Decreased serum chloride, Increased serum bicarbonate (Choice B)}}

Step 1: Identify the relationship between P-waves and QRS complexes  
- On the tracing you see more P waves than QRS complexes (i.e. occasional P waves fail to conduct → dropped QRS). This is a second-degree AV block.

Step 2: Look at the PR intervals before the dropped beat  
- The PR intervals of the conducted beats are constant (no progressive prolongation).  

Step 3: Classify the block  
- Constant PR intervals with intermittent nonconducted P waves = Mobitz type II (second-degree) AV block.

Step 4: Correlate clinically  
- Mobitz II blocks are infra-nodal and unpredictable, often leading to syncope and requiring pacing.  

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Mobitz type II (second-degree) atrioventricular block}}

Step 1: Identify the core behavioral symptoms  
– The child is “extremely active,” “runs wildly in the classroom,” “talks excessively,” “gets out of his seat,” and “often forgets to turn in his homework.”  
– These symptoms have been present for at least a year and are causing problems at school.

Step 2: Match these symptoms to DSM-5 criteria  
Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) requires a persistent pattern (≥6 months) of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity, with onset before age 12, and impairment in at least two settings (e.g., school and home).  
– Inattention: forgets to turn in homework  
– Hyperactivity/impulsivity: runs/climbs excessively, talks excessively, leaves seat  

Step 3: Explain the frequent injuries  
Children with ADHD are more prone to accidental injuries because of their impulsivity and poor risk assessment. Multiple fractures, bruises, and dislocations in an active, inattentive child are more consistent with ADHD than with nonaccidental trauma or another psychiatric diagnosis.

Step 4: Rule out the other options  
• Conduct disorder would involve deliberate rule-breaking, aggression toward people/animals, or theft—not merely impulsive, accidental injuries.  
• A learning disorder affects academic skills (reading, math, writing) without the pervasive hyperactivity/impulsivity.  
• A seizure disorder would present with episodic neurological changes (e.g., postictal state), not chronic hyperactivity.  
• Age-appropriate behavior would not account for such marked impairment at school and home or the long history of injuries.

Final Answer: \boxed{A\ \text{Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder}}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical picture  
– A patient with early Lyme disease (a spirochetal infection) starts doxycycline and within a few hours develops fever, chills, hypotension, tachycardia, myalgias, and headache.  
– These findings are classic for the Jarisch–Herxheimer reaction, seen when spirochetes are rapidly killed by antibiotics.

Step 2: Understand the pathophysiology  
– Doxycycline kills Borrelia burgdorferi, causing the release of spirochetal lipoproteins and other proinflammatory bacterial products.  
– These bacterial products trigger an acute inflammatory response mediated by cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8.  
– Clinically, this presents with fever, chills, hypotension, tachycardia, and worsening myalgias/headache shortly after antibiotic initiation.

Step 3: Rule out the other answer choices  
A. Exacerbation of infection by B. burgdorferi – Incorrect. The patient’s symptoms are due to an inflammatory response to dying bacteria, not to uncontrolled infection.  
B. Infection-mediated sepsis – Incorrect. Sepsis implies a progressive overwhelming infection; here the timing (within hours of antibiotic) and lack of a new infectious source point to Jarisch–Herxheimer, not sepsis.  
C. IgE-mediated allergic reaction to doxycycline – Incorrect. IgE reactions cause urticaria, bronchospasm, angioedema, and typically occur within minutes, not hours.  
E. Secretion of bacterial endotoxins – Incorrect. Borrelia (a spirochete) does not have classic endotoxin (LPS) in its outer membrane; the mediator is spirochetal lipoprotein release.

Step 4: Select the mechanism  
– The Jarisch–Herxheimer reaction is driven by the release of bacterial products (lipoproteins) that induce acute inflammation.

Final Answer: \boxed{Release\ of\ bacterial\ products\ producing\ acute\ inflammation}

Step 1: Recognize the context and red flags for malingering  
• The patient is in a medicolegal context (suing his employer for PTSD).  
• He is reading his symptoms from a written list, suggesting they may be rehearsed.  
• He has clear external incentives (financial compensation).  

Step 2: Understand why other options are inappropriate  
A. Amobarbital interview (“truth serum”) is neither reliable nor ethically acceptable.  
C. Hypnosis is not recommended for medicolegal evaluations and may produce confabulations.  
D. Focusing on eye contact is not a validated method to differentiate genuine from feigned PTSD.  
E. Directly accusing him of malingering is premature and may damage the therapeutic relationship.  

Step 3: Choose the best approach  
• The most appropriate next step is to obtain an unstructured, open‐ended narrative.  
• Asking the patient to describe, in his own words, the traumatic event and his symptoms allows you to look for inconsistencies, gaps, or implausible details that often characterize feigned presentations.

Final Answer: \boxed{B}

Step 1: Identify the ethical principles involved  
- The patient is a competent adult and therefore has the right to refuse recommended treatment (respect for autonomy).  
- The physician’s duty of beneficence requires recommending therapies that are supported by evidence and standard of care (lumpectomy + radiation).  
- The physician must maintain a therapeutic alliance and avoid abandoning the patient (nonmaleficence, fidelity).

Step 2: Evaluate the response options  
A. “There is no proof…you’re throwing your life away.”  
  - Judgmental and paternalistic; undermines trust.  
B. “Agree to the alternative therapies…see you in 6 months.”  
  - Endorses unproven, potentially harmful delay.  Six months is too long to defer standard treatment for a small malignancy.  
C. “Would you return in 2 weeks to discuss treatment options again?”  
  - Respects autonomy, keeps the door open, allows the patient time to process and make an informed decision, and enables early re-evaluation.  
D. “I’ll refer you to another physician.”  
  - Effectively abandons the patient and fails to provide adequate continuing care.

Step 3: Choose the most appropriate response  
Option C is best because it:  
  • Acknowledges her right to choose while gently encouraging reconsideration of proven therapy  
  • Maintains the physician–patient relationship and continuity of care  
  • Avoids long delays that could worsen prognosis

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Ask the patient if she would be willing to return to the office in 2 weeks to discuss treatment options again.}}

Step 1: Identify the clinical scenario  
– A patient has an expected but incomplete recovery (decreased range of motion 4 months after Dupuytren contracture repair).  
– He believes his physician is at fault and files a malpractice suit.

Step 2: Recall key risk factors for malpractice claims  
Studies show that the single greatest precipitant of malpractice suits is a breakdown in the doctor–patient relationship, most often due to poor communication or a perceived lack of caring.  
Other factors (patient socioeconomic status, physician experience, ability to screen difficult patients) play much smaller roles.

Step 3: Match the answer choices  
A. “Physician is incompetent” – patients rarely use legal action just for perceived incompetence without communication breakdown.  
B. “Physician is uncaring” – poor bedside manner and lack of empathy are strongly linked to malpractice suits.  
C. Socioeconomic status – may influence care, but not the primary precipitant of litigation.  
D. Physician’s experience – less important than communication skills.  
E. Physician’s screening of problem patients – not a major factor in suit initiation.

Final Answer: the most likely precipitating factor is the patient’s perception that the physician is uncaring.

Step 2: Consider interventions at different levels

- System level (e.g., protocols, order sets, standing orders)

A. Nursing in‐service on DVT prophylaxis  
  • Improves knowledge but does not reliably change ordering behavior or ensure every at‐risk patient gets prophylaxis.  
B. Institutional policy with standardized orders for DVT prophylaxis  
  • Embeds prophylaxis into routine post‐op order sets, minimizes reliance on individual recall, and aligns practice with evidence‐based guidelines.  
C. Encourage early ambulation  
  • Helpful but alone is insufficient for high‐risk cancer surgery patients who need pharmacologic or mechanical prophylaxis.  
D. Prescribe LMWH for all postoperative patients  
  • Overly broad (some surgeries are low risk) and may increase bleeding risk inappropriately.  
E. No intervention

- Standardized order sets (B) ensure that all qualifying patients automatically receive appropriate prophylaxis, reducing omissions and improving safety.

Step 1: Identify the lesion  
- Acute onset (24 h) of right‐sided weakness involving the frontalis (forehead), orbicularis oculi, orbicularis oris, buccinator, and platysma muscles → this is a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial palsy (both upper and lower face involved).  
- Hyperacusis (sound louder on the right) indicates stapedius muscle involvement (facial nerve).  
- Preceding mild ear ache (“prodrome”) is common in idiopathic facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy).  
- No other neurologic deficits and normal ear/oral exam further support isolated facial nerve involvement.  

Step 2: Exclude other causes  
- In a classic presentation of Bell’s palsy (acute, LMN facial paralysis with the typical prodrome and no “red flags”), further imaging (MRI, angiography) or CSF analysis is not routinely required.  

Step 3: Understand the natural history and prognosis  
- Bell’s palsy is idiopathic facial nerve palsy.  
- Without treatment, about 70–85% of patients recover complete or near‐complete function within 3–12 months.  
- Early corticosteroid therapy (if started within 72 h) can modestly increase the chance of complete recovery, but spontaneous recovery rates are already high.  

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
A. Cerebral angiography is not needed and does not affect prognosis.  
B. MRI/CSF analysis is not needed in a classic case and does not change the expected recovery.  
C. “She has an 80% chance of complete recovery within the next 12 months without treatment.” → This matches the known spontaneous recovery rate of Bell’s palsy (~70–85%).  
D. Corticosteroids improve recovery rates above spontaneous levels, so only a 20% chance with steroids is incorrect.  
E. It is very likely she will recover; this statement is false.  

Final answer:  
\boxed{She has an 80\% chance of complete recovery within the next 12 months without treatment}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical context  
– A mechanically ventilated COPD patient had a right‐sided pneumothorax treated with a chest tube.  
– An “air leak” was noted for 24 h (indicating ongoing communication between airway and pleural space), but the leak then stopped.  

Step 2: Identify the new signs of deterioration  
– Restlessness and combativeness (signs of respiratory distress or hypoxia)  
– Diminished right‐sided breath sounds (suggesting recurrence of air in pleural space)  
– Tachycardia but stable blood pressure (early hemodynamic effects)  

Step 3: Formulate the most likely problem  
– The sudden cessation of the air‐leak plus new diminished breath sounds and distress strongly suggests that the chest tube is no longer effectively evacuating air (tube may be malpositioned, kinked, plugged, or dislodged), allowing reaccumulation of a (possibly tension) pneumothorax.

Step 4: Choose the most appropriate immediate intervention  
– While obtaining a chest X‐ray is reasonable, the patient is showing signs of acute respiratory compromise. The priority is to restore pleural drainage.  
– Repositioning or otherwise correcting the chest tube (ensuring it is patent, correctly placed, and connected to suction) is the fastest way to relieve the trapped air and reverse the pneumothorax.  

Other options are inappropriate because:  
– Adding PEEP (A) risks worsening intrapleural pressure.  
– β-blockers (B) or alprazolam (C) treat neither the mechanical problem nor the distress.  
– Bag‐mask ventilation without a functional chest tube (D) can force more air into the pleural space, exacerbating a tension pneumothorax.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Reposition the chest tube (Choice E)}}

Step 1: Identify the key features  
• The patient started metronidazole 3 days ago for Trichomonas.  
• Last night she drank alcohol (champagne).  
• Over the past 12 hours she has had nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, and mild flushing (pallor/dry mucous membranes can accompany the hypotension and vasodilation).  

Step 2: Recall metronidazole’s disulfiram‐like effect  
• Metronidazole (like disulfiram) inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase.  
• This leads to accumulation of acetaldehyde when alcohol is ingested.  
• Acetaldehyde buildup causes nausea, vomiting, flushing, tachycardia, and hypotension.  

Step 3: Exclude the other choices  
B. Alcohol‐induced gastritis would cause epigastric pain and maybe bleeding but not this acute constellation after metronidazole.  
C. Metformin–associated lactic acidosis presents with hyperventilation, metabolic acidosis, and altered mental status—unlikely here.  
D. Metronidazole hepatotoxicity is rare and develops over longer time with elevated LFTs.  
E. Viral hepatitis develops over weeks, not acutely after one night of drinking.  

Final Answer  
The patient’s presentation is best explained by a disulfiram‐like reaction from metronidazole leading to accumulation of acetaldehyde.  

\boxed{A}

- MCI = subjective memory complaint + objective memory deficit on testing + preserved general cognitive function and preserved activities of daily living.

Step 4: Management  
- No additional diagnostic studies are indicated at this time.

Step 1: Clinical presentation  
– A 75-year-old with a long (3-year) history of hand/wrist pain, worsened over the past few months, with difficulty in fine movements (buttoning her coat).

Step 2: Exam findings (from the photograph)  
– Bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints (Heberden’s nodes) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints (Bouchard’s nodes).  
– No obvious soft-tissue swelling or erythema.

Step 3: Differential diagnosis  
– Osteoarthritis (OA): Older age, pain worse with use, bony enlargement in DIP/PIP, presence of Heberden’s and Bouchard’s nodes.  
– Rheumatoid arthritis (RA): Typically involves PIP/MCP joints with synovitis, ulnar deviation, symmetrical swelling, morning stiffness >1 hr.

Step 4: Pathophysiology of OA  
– Progressive degeneration of articular (hyaline) cartilage  
– Subchondral bone sclerosis and cyst formation  
– Osteophyte (bone spur) formation at joint margins  
– Joint space narrowing

Step 5: Match with answer choices  
A. Cortical thinning – seen in osteoporosis, not OA  
B. Degenerative changes of the cartilage – hallmark of OA  
C. Inflammation of digital tendons – more tendonitis or RA finding  
D. Neutrophilic infiltration of the synovium – acute arthritis (e.g., gout), RA has lymphocytes, plasma cells  
E. Thickening of the synovium with pannus – RA  

Thus the finding most consistent with osteoarthritis is degenerative (wear-and-tear) changes of the articular cartilage.  

Final answer: \boxed{B}.

Step 1: Characterize the murmurs  
- A grade 2/6 systolic “ejection” murmur heard at the upper left sternal border (pulmonic area) suggests increased flow across the pulmonary valve.  
- A soft mid-diastolic rumble at the lower left sternal border suggests increased flow across the tricuspid valve.  

Step 2: Analyze the splitting of S2  
- S2 is “widely split” and does *not* vary with respiration (“fixed” split).  
- Fixed splitting reflects a constant delay in right ventricular emptying, independent of breathing.  

Step 3: Integrate findings  
- Increased right‐heart flow → louder pulmonic ejection murmur + mid-diastolic tricuspid rumble.  
- Fixed wide splitting of S2.  
- No signs of left‐sided volume overload or cyanosis.  

Step 4: Match to congenital lesions  
- Atrial septal defect (ASD), especially ostium secundum type, classically causes:  
  • Fixed wide S2 split  
  • Pulmonic systolic ejection murmur  
  • Mid-diastolic rumble at the tricuspid area  

Other lesions listed (aortic stenosis, VSD, PDA, etc.) do not produce fixed splitting of S2 with this combination of murmurs in an asymptomatic child.

Final Answer:  
\boxed{\text{Atrial septal defect (ASD)}}

Step 1: Oligohydramnios in utero is most often due to decreased fetal urine output (amniotic fluid after the first trimester is > 90% fetal urine).  
Step 2: Absence of a urethral outlet (e.g. urethral atresia or posterior urethral valves in a male fetus) causes urinary retention and essentially no urine enters the amniotic sac.  
Step 3: Amniotic fluid is critical for normal lung development—without it, the lungs cannot expand and develop their alveolar and vascular networks.  
Step 4: The classic consequence of severe oligohydramnios is pulmonary hypoplasia (underdevelopment of the lungs).  

Hence, the additional finding most likely in this stillborn is pulmonary hypoplasia.

Final Answer: \boxed{Pulmonary\ hypoplasia} (Choice C)

Step 1: Identify key clinical features  
- Acute (1-day) onset of widespread painful skin exfoliation with serous oozing  
- Involvement of mucous membranes (erythema and exfoliation in the mouth)  
- Recent drug exposure (allopurinol and probenecid for 2 weeks)

Step 2: Consider the main causes of blistering/exfoliative skin disease  
- Bullous pemphigoid: chronic, tense bullae, usually spares mucosa  
- Pemphigus erythematosus: variant of pemphigus with superficial blisters, positive Nikolsky sign, but less mucosal and not classically drug‐triggered  
- Staphylococcal scalded‐skin syndrome (SSSS): typically in children, spares mucous membranes, caused by staphylococcal exotoxin  
- Erythema multiforme (EM) and Stevens–Johnson syndrome (SJS)/toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN): EM has target lesions; SJS/TEN are drug‐related, involve mucosa

Step 3: Differentiate SJS vs. TEN  
- Both are part of a spectrum of severe mucocutaneous drug reactions  
- Stevens–Johnson syndrome: <10% body surface area (BSA) epidermal detachment  
- Toxic epidermal necrolysis: >30% BSA epidermal detachment, diffuse exfoliation  

Step 4: Apply to this patient  
- Diffuse exfoliation (implying large BSA)  
- Mucosal involvement  
- No target lesions (rules out erythema multiforme)  
- Drug exposure 1–3 weeks prior (typical for SJS/TEN)

Conclusion: This presentation is classic for toxic epidermal necrolysis.

Final answer: \boxed{Toxic\ epidermal\ necrolysis}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical picture  
• A previously healthy adolescent develops sudden headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, confusion, hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnea, diaphoresis, and a core temperature of 39.5 °C during full‐contact football practice on a hot day.  
• The combination of hyperthermia and altered mental status after exertion is diagnostic of exertional heat stroke.

Step 2: Know the priorities in management of exertional heat stroke  
• The two immediate threats are (1) hyperthermia with end‐organ damage (especially CNS) and (2) hypovolemic shock from dehydration and vasodilation.  
• Standard teaching emphasizes rapid cooling (ice‐water immersion is the most effective method) and aggressive circulatory support (isotonic IV fluids).

Step 3: Choose the single most appropriate next step  
• Although ice‐water immersion is the gold‐standard cooling method, this patient is hypotensive (BP 90/65) and at risk for cardiovascular collapse.  
• The very first step is to restore intravascular volume with isotonic crystalloid, which also promotes heat loss and stabilizes perfusion. Once IV access and fluid resuscitation are underway, rapid cooling measures can proceed in parallel.

Final answer: \boxed{Administer\ intravenous\ fluids\ (option\ C)}

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– A 35-year-old man with acute onset low-back pain radiating down the posterior aspect of the right leg, made worse by straight‐leg raising, and associated with loss of the right ankle (S1) reflex → classic signs of an S1 radiculopathy (sciatica).

Step 2: Recognize the most common cause in this setting  
– Acute lifting injury → most likely an intervertebral disc herniation.  
– Specifically, a posterolateral herniation of the nucleus pulposus at the L5–S1 level compressing the traversing S1 nerve root.

Step 3: Correlate pathophysiology with answer choices  
A. Displacement of the nucleus pulposus → disc herniation compressing a nerve root → fits acute sciatica.  
B. Hypertrophy of the facet joints → contributes to spinal stenosis in older patients, causes neurogenic claudication rather than acute radiculopathy.  
C. Osteophyte formation → degenerative spondylosis, more chronic presentation.  
D. Spondylolisthesis → forward slippage of one vertebra on another, may cause back pain but less commonly acute radiculopathy after a lifting injury.  
E. Thickening of ligamentum flavum → contributes to spinal canal narrowing in chronic degenerative disease.

Step 4: Conclude the most likely mechanism  
– The acute lifting injury caused displacement (herniation) of the nucleus pulposus, resulting in compression of the S1 nerve root and the patient’s sciatica.

Final Answer: \boxed{A}  Displacement of the nucleus pulposus.

Step 1: Classify the discharge  
– This patient has a new, spontaneous, unilateral, non-milky nipple discharge.  Although it is described as “black” rather than frankly bloody, it clearly is not the physiologic (galactorrhea) type.  Any spontaneous, unilateral discharge from a single duct—even if dark or “coffee-ground” in appearance—must be considered pathologic until proven otherwise.  

Step 2: Initial imaging  
– The first diagnostic step in pathologic nipple discharge is targeted breast imaging (diagnostic mammogram ± ultrasound).  However, this patient underwent mammography and ultrasound only two months ago, with benign findings and a recommended six-month follow-up.  Because her imaging was recent and showed no suspicious masses, we proceed to the next evaluation.  

Step 3: Ductography  
– When pathologic discharge persists despite negative or benign prior imaging, the next test is ductography (galactography).  This involves cannulating the involved duct, injecting contrast, and taking detailed mammographic views to localize any intraductal filling defects (for example, papillomas or early malignancies).  

Step 4: Surgical excision  
– If ductography identifies a lesion, or if the discharge persists without clear ductographic findings, the definitive diagnostic—and often therapeutic—step is microdochectomy (excisional biopsy of the involved duct).  

Because this patient’s pathologic discharge has already undergone recent benign imaging, the most appropriate next diagnostic step is ductography.  

Final answer:  \boxed{\text{Ductography}}

Step 1: Clinical clues  
– 4-year-old boy from Brazil with a rapidly growing, painless jaw mass  
– Photomicrograph shows a “starry‐sky” pattern.

Step 2: Diagnosis  
– Burkitt lymphoma classically presents in African (endemic) children as a jaw mass and shows a “starry‐sky” appearance (sheets of monotonous lymphocytes with interspersed macrophages).

Step 3: Histologic correlate of the arrows  
– The arrows point to macrophages containing little fragments of apoptotic debris (“tingible‐body macrophages”).

Step 4: Underlying cellular process  
– Those debris‐laden macrophages are clearing apoptotic cells; apoptosis is characterized by cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, membrane blebbing, and phagocytosis without inflammation.

Answer  
The process indicated by the arrows is  apoptosis.

Final answer: \boxed{Apoptosis}

Step 1: Identify the physician’s emotional reaction  
The physician is expressing frustration and shame toward the patient (“You should be ashamed…we’ve all worked so hard to keep you healthy”), which reflects the doctor’s own feelings rather than the patient’s.

Step 2: Review the defense mechanisms/transference concepts  
A. Countertransference – the physician’s unconscious emotional response to the patient based on the physician’s own conflicts or feelings.  
B. Identification with the aggressor – taking on characteristics of someone perceived as hostile to reduce anxiety.  
C. Projection – attributing one’s own unacceptable feelings to someone else.  
D. Splitting – seeing people or situations as all good or all bad.  
E. Sublimation – redirecting unacceptable impulses into socially acceptable activities.

Step 3: Match the scenario to the definition  
Here, the physician’s negative reaction is driven by her own frustrations (including difficulties with her son) and is being projected onto the patient. This is a classic example of countertransference.

Final answer: \boxed{Countertransference}

Step 1: Identify the therapies and their primary adverse‐effect profiles  
• Nicotine patch  
  – Delivers steady, lower‐peak levels of nicotine than cigarettes  
  – Main side effects: local skin irritation, vivid dreams/insomnia, mild tachycardia  
  – Does not markedly worsen hypertension or renal function at therapeutic doses  
• Bupropion  
  – An atypical antidepressant that inhibits norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake  
  – Lowers the seizure threshold (risk of seizure ↑ especially in patients with eating disorders, abrupt alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal, or preexisting seizure disorders)  
  – Does not directly cause cold intolerance, polyuria, or renal insufficiency  

Step 2: Review the patient’s seizure history  
• Childhood febrile seizures (usually benign; does not constitute an ongoing seizure disorder)  
• No history of epilepsy or adult‐onset seizures → bupropion is not contraindicated in this patient  

Step 3: Evaluate each answer choice  
A. Cold intolerance – not associated with either agent  
B. Hypertension – nicotine patch can cause mild tachycardia but usually does not cause significant hypertension; bupropion has no major pressor effect  
C. Polyuria – not associated with either agent  
D. Renal insufficiency – neither agent causes renal failure in a healthy young adult  
E. No increased risk – correct, since the patient has no true seizure disorder and neither drug combination predisposes him to the other listed adverse events  

Final answer: \boxed{E} the patient is at no increased risk.

Step 1: Assess renal function labs  
- BUN = 78 mg/dL, Cr = 3.2 mg/dL → BUN/Cr ≈ 24 (normal ≤ 15)  
- Urine Na⁺ = 8 mEq/L (low)  
- Urine specific gravity = 1.030 (high concentration)  

Step 2: Calculate FeNa  
FeNa ≈ (UNa × PCr)/(PNa × UCr)  
≈ (8 × 3.2)/(131 × 90) ≈ 0.002 (0.2%), well below 1%

Step 3: Interpret values  
- BUN/Cr > 20  
- FeNa < 1%  
- High urine specific gravity  

→ Findings are diagnostic of prerenal azotemia

Step 4: Identify cause in this patient  
He’s febrile with rhinorrhea/cough and poor oral intake → intravascular volume depletion leading to prerenal azotemia

Final answer: \boxed{Decreased\ intravascular\ volume}

Step 1: Identify the key clinical context  
– A 45-year-old woman with systemic sclerosis presenting with progressive dyspnea and a nonproductive cough.  

Step 2: Interpret the physical exam and PFT findings  
– Crackles at both lung bases (“Velcro” crackles) suggest interstitial involvement.  
– Total lung capacity at 80% of predicted indicates a mild restrictive defect.  
– Diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO) reduced to 65% of predicted indicates impaired gas exchange across the alveolar–capillary membrane.  

Step 3: Recall pulmonary complications of systemic sclerosis  
– Systemic sclerosis commonly leads to interstitial pulmonary fibrosis (a form of restrictive lung disease).  

Step 4: Correlate histologic expectations  
– Interstitial pulmonary fibrosis is characterized histologically by diffuse interstitial fibrosis with fibroblastic foci, architectural distortion, and possibly honeycomb changes.  

Step 5: Eliminate other choices  
B. Intra-alveolar exudates – seen in pneumonia/acute respiratory distress syndrome  
C. Multiple thromboemboli – would present with acute dyspnea and V/Q mismatch, not chronic fibrosis  
D. Necrotizing vasculitis – seen in granulomatosis with polyangiitis  
E. Non-necrotizing interstitial granulomas – seen in sarcoidosis  

Final Answer:  
\boxed{Diffuse\ interstitial\ fibrosis}

Step 1. Identify event rates in each group  
• Experimental (aspirin) event rate = 1.2% = 0.012  
• Control (placebo) event rate    = 2.2% = 0.022  

Step 2. Compute the absolute risk reduction (ARR)  
ARR = Control event rate − Experimental event rate  
    = 0.022 − 0.012  
    = 0.010 = 1.0%  

Step 3. Compute the relative risk reduction (RRR) for completeness  
RRR = ARR / Control event rate  
    = 0.010 / 0.022  
    ≈ 0.455 = 45.5%  

Step 4. Compute the number needed to treat (NNT) for completeness  
NNT = 1 / ARR  
    = 1 / 0.010  
    = 100  

Step 5. Match to the answer choices  
• ARR = 1.0% → choice A  
• RRR ≈ 45.5% (no matching choice)  
• NNT = 100 (no matching choice)  

Therefore, the most accurate interpretation is:  
\boxed{Absolute\ risk\ reduction\ is\ 1\%}

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– A 65-year-old asthmatic develops bronchospasm, hypotension, and wheezing within minutes of taking aspirin (and previously ibuprofen).  
– This is classic aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD), a pseudoallergic reaction, not IgE-mediated.

Step 2: Recall the relevant biochemistry  
– Aspirin (and other nonselective NSAIDs) irreversibly inhibit cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2).  
– COX enzymes normally convert arachidonic acid into prostaglandins (PGs), prostacyclin (PGI₂), thromboxane A₂, and endoperoxide intermediates.  
– When COX is blocked, arachidonic acid is shunted into the 5-lipoxygenase pathway, leading to overproduction of leukotrienes (especially LTC₄, LTD₄, and LTE₄).

Step 3: Link mediator to symptoms  
– Leukotrienes C₄, D₄, and E₄ are potent bronchoconstrictors and increase vascular permeability.  
– Their accumulation causes acute bronchospasm and hypotension seen in this patient.

Step 4: Exclude the other choices  
A. Endoperoxides (PGH₂) – transient intermediates in PG synthesis, not overproduced when COX is blocked  
C. Prostacyclin (PGI₂) – decreased by COX inhibition; normally vasodilatory and antiplatelet  
D. Prostaglandins – likewise decreased by aspirin  
E. Thromboxane A₂ – decreased by aspirin; normally vasoconstrictive and pro-aggregatory  

Final answer: \boxed{Leukotrienes (choice B)}

Step 1: Characterize his hypoxemia.  
– Despite 100% O₂ by nonrebreather mask, his PaO₂ remains extremely low (50 mm Hg).  Such “refractory” hypoxemia that does not correct with high–FiO₂ points to a true shunt physiology (areas of lung are perfused but not ventilated).

Step 2: Review the chest x-ray and clinical setting.  
– Bilateral infiltrates in a young man with fever, productive cough, and acute respiratory distress are most consistent with severe pneumonia (or ARDS); consolidated or fluid-filled alveoli cannot participate in gas exchange.

Step 3: Recall the causes of hypoxemia.  
1. Low FiO₂ – not the case here (he’s on 100% O₂).  
2. Hypoventilation – would raise PaCO₂ (here it’s normal).  
3. Diffusion limitation – usually seen in interstitial lung disease; partially correctable with O₂.  
4. V/Q mismatch – improved by O₂, unless very severe.  
5. Shunt (V/Q = 0) – not improved by 100% O₂.

Step 4: Identify the best match.  
– Consolidated alveolar units are perfused but not ventilated → an intrapulmonary shunt → refractory hypoxemia.

Final answer: \boxed{Ventilation\text{–}perfusion\ mismatch\ and\ shunt\ (Choice\ D).}

Step 1: Identify the disorder  
– The patient has an incidental adrenal mass plus persistently elevated blood pressure.  
– Laboratory studies show elevated urinary metanephrines and plasma aldosterone levels; the markedly elevated metanephrines point to a pheochromocytoma.

Step 2: Understand preoperative risks  
– Manipulation of a pheochromocytoma during surgery can precipitate massive catecholamine release, leading to hypertensive crises, arrhythmias, and end-organ damage.  
– Proper preoperative preparation is essential to block the effects of excessive catecholamines.

Step 3: Choose the correct preoperative blockade  
– First, administer a nonselective, irreversible α-adrenergic antagonist (phenoxybenzamine) for 10–14 days to control blood pressure and prevent intraoperative hypertension.  
– Only after adequate α-blockade is achieved should a β-blocker be added (if needed) to control reflex tachycardia.  
– Agents such as labetalol (mixed α/β blocker) or nifedipine (calcium-channel blocker) are not first-line for pheochromocytoma preparation, and spironolactone or potassium loading are inappropriate in this context.

Final answer: \boxed{Phenoxybenzamine}

Step 1: Identify the key clinical features  
- A 2-year-old with acute onset of severe pain, swelling, and redness of one finger (thumb)  
- No history of trauma  
- Low-grade fever (37.7°C)  
- Oral vesicle and cervical lymphadenopathy  

Step 2: Recognize the characteristic lesion on the finger  
- The photo (not shown here) is described as an erythematous swollen digit with clustered vesicles/pustules (“dew-drop on a rose petal” appearance typical for herpes)  

Step 3: Correlate with herpetic whitlow  
- Herpetic whitlow presents as a painful vesicular eruption on a finger, often accompanied by fever, lymphadenopathy, and possible oral lesions  
- Commonly caused by HSV-1 in children (HSV-2 in healthcare workers via genital lesions)  

Step 4: Determine the type of pathogen  
- Herpes simplex virus is an enveloped, double-stranded DNA virus belonging to the Herpesviridae family  

Final Answer: \boxed{DNA\ virus}

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– A 14-year-old obese boy with insidious onset of hip (and referred knee) pain, worsened by weight bearing.  
– Physical exam shows limited hip abduction, internal rotation, and flexion on the affected side.  
– This presentation is classic for slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE).

Step 2: Recall risk factors for SCFE  
– Obesity (most common)  
– Adolescent growth spurt (typically ages 10–16)  
– Male sex  
– Endocrine disorders (hypothyroidism, growth hormone therapy)  
– African-American ethnicity  
– Less important factors: minor trauma, family history.

Step 3: Match the patient’s history to the risk factors listed  
A. BMI → He is obese (BMI > 95th percentile), a major risk factor for SCFE.  
B. Family history → His mother has type II diabetes and grandmother has osteoporosis; neither is linked to SCFE.  
C. Medication use → Inhaled corticosteroids have minimal systemic effect; not a known SCFE risk.  
D. Previous fractures → A remote tibia/fibula fracture is unrelated to SCFE risk.  
E. Recent physical activity → Increased walking may precipitate symptoms but does not cause the slip.

Step 4: Choose the factor that most increased his risk  
– Obesity (elevated BMI) is the strongest and most well-established risk factor for SCFE.

Final answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize an acute focal neurologic deficit in a patient with risk factors for cerebrovascular disease (atrial fibrillation, hypertension, diabetes) and a 12‐hour history of aphasia plus contralateral motor and sensory loss—findings consistent with an acute stroke.  
Step 2: The first diagnostic study in any suspected acute stroke is a noncontrast head CT, which differentiates ischemic from hemorrhagic stroke and guides further management (eg, thrombolysis).  
Step 3: Other studies (eg, carotid duplex, cerebral angiography) are secondary and would follow the initial CT; EEG and lumbar puncture are not indicated in this presentation.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{CT scan of the head}}

Step 1: Clinical findings  
– Trauma with an open popliteal‐fossa wound, ecchymosis above the knee  
– Absent popliteal and distal tibial pulses on the right, with motor and sensory deficits below the knee  
– These findings localize to an injury of the popliteal artery  

Step 2: Arteriogram interpretation  
– The arteriogram shows an abrupt cutoff of contrast flow in the popliteal artery just below the knee, with no distal reconstitution  
– This pattern indicates an intimal tear or transection of the popliteal artery rather than a simple spasm (which shows smooth narrowing)  

Step 3: Correlate with mechanism  
– Knee dislocations, particularly posterior dislocations, are notorious for injuring the popliteal artery by stretching or tearing it against the femoral condyles  
– Posterior dislocations are far more common than anterior ones in high‐velocity trauma and typically give this vascular picture  

Step 4: Exclude other choices  
– Arterial spasm would show tapered narrowing without an abrupt cutoff  
– Femur or tibia/fibula fractures can injure vessels, but the classic arterial injury with immediate limb ischemia and an abrupt angiographic cutoff is seen with knee dislocation  

Final Answer  
\boxed{Posterior\ dislocation\ of\ the\ right\ knee}

Step 1: Identify the lipid abnormalities and clinical findings  
- Total cholesterol is 410 mg/dL (markedly elevated)  
- Triglycerides are 140 mg/dL (near normal)  
- Tendinous xanthomas (especially Achilles)  
- Strong family history of early myocardial infarction  

Step 2: Match to a specific dyslipidemia  
- Markedly elevated LDL (reflected in high total cholesterol with normal TG)  
- Tendon xanthomas are characteristic of familial hypercholesterolemia (Type IIa)  

Step 3: Recall the molecular defect in familial hypercholesterolemia  
- Type IIa is caused by a defect in the LDL receptor, leading to decreased clearance of LDL  

Step 4: Eliminate the other choices  
A. Apo B48 – required for chylomicron formation; deficiency → fat malabsorption, not isolated ↑LDL  
B. Apo C – cofactor for lipoprotein lipase; deficiency → hypertriglyceridemia (Type I), not ↑LDL  
C. HMG-CoA reductase activity – decreased activity would lower cholesterol synthesis, not raise it  
E. Lipoprotein lipase activity – deficiency causes severe hypertriglyceridemia, not isolated ↑LDL  

Final Answer: \boxed{LDL\ receptor\ (choice\ D)}

Step 1: Recall what a correlation coefficient measures  
- A correlation (r) quantifies the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two continuous variables.  
- r ranges from –1 to +1.  
  - r > 0 indicates a positive association (both variables tend to increase together).  
  - r < 0 indicates a negative association (one variable tends to decrease as the other increases).  
  - |r| near 1 indicates a strong linear relationship; |r| near 0 indicates little or no linear relationship.

Step 2: Interpret r = 0.6 for LDL and hs-CRP  
- r = +0.6 is a moderate positive correlation.  
- This means that, in the study population, individuals with higher LDL levels tend also to have higher hs-CRP levels.

Step 3: Distinguish association from causation  
- Correlation does not prove causation.  
- Although LDL and hs-CRP rise together, we cannot conclude one causes the other based on a correlation coefficient alone.

Step 4: Match to the answer choices  
A. “Higher LDL concentrations are associated with higher hs-CRP concentrations” – correctly describes a positive correlation without implying causation.  
B. Says “associated with lower” – incorrect (it’s positive, not negative).  
C. and D. Imply causation – incorrect interpretation of correlation.

Final Answer:
\boxed{A\text{. Higher LDL concentrations are associated with higher hs-CRP concentrations}}

Step 1: Assess the clinical picture  
– 23-year-old with septic shock from pneumonia, already intubated, on broad-spectrum antibiotics, sedated and ventilated, and has received an initial 4 L of crystalloid.  
– Despite this, he remains hypotensive (79/43 mm Hg) and tachycardic (135 bpm).

Step 2: Evaluate fluid responsiveness  
– Bedside US: IVC diameter 1.8 cm with minimal respirophasic collapsibility → high CVP, “full” IVC, suggesting he is no longer fluid responsive.  
– Cardiac echo: Normal/high EF (∼ 65%) → adequate contractility, arguing against cardiogenic shock.

Step 3: Exclude other causes  
– Labs: WBC and lactate are improving but hemodynamics remain poor → ongoing vasodilatory shock.  
– No signs of bleeding, tamponade, or tension pneumothorax.  
– No focal neurologic findings to warrant immediate head CT.  
– No evidence of invasive fungal infection to justify voriconazole.  
– Steroids (hydrocortisone) reserved for refractory shock after vasopressors.

Step 4: Next best step  
– He has septic (distributive) shock with adequate volume resuscitation and normal cardiac function.  
– First‐line vasopressor in septic shock is norepinephrine.

Final Answer  
\boxed{\text{Begin a continuous infusion of norepinephrine.}}

Step 1: Identify the key clinical findings.  
– Acute onset of right‐sided abdominal/flank pain radiating to the back  
– CT shows an enlarged kidney with wedge‐shaped hypodense areas (no stones)  

Step 2: Interpret the CT findings.  
– Wedge‐shaped hypodensities in the renal parenchyma are classic for renal infarction due to arterial occlusion  

Step 3: Determine the most likely mechanism.  
– Renal infarction is usually caused by an embolus lodging in a segmental renal artery branch  

Step 4: Review this patient’s risk factors for thromboembolism.  
– Atrial fibrillation → stasis of blood in the atria → predisposes to thrombus formation → systemic embolization to the kidney  
– Other choices (hyperlipidemia, hypertension, smoking, diabetes) are mainly risk factors for chronic atherosclerotic disease, not for acute arterial embolism  

Answer: The strongest predisposing risk factor is his atrial fibrillation.  

Final answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize the event as a TIA  
– A transient episode of slurred speech and right-hand clumsiness lasting <24 hours (45 minutes) with full resolution is diagnostic of a transient ischemic attack (TIA).

Step 2: Initiate immediate secondary‐prevention measures  
– He has already been started on daily aspirin (antiplatelet therapy), which is appropriate for TIA.

Step 3: Identify and rank modifiable risk factors for stroke  
– The most important modifiable risk factor for both first and recurrent stroke is hypertension.  
– Other risk factors include hyperlipidemia, smoking, obesity, and excessive alcohol use, but blood pressure control has the greatest impact on reducing stroke risk.

Step 4: Choose the next intervention  
– His blood pressure is 170/90 mm Hg, well above the target of <140/90 mm Hg for patients post-TIA.  
– Initiating antihypertensive therapy will substantially lower his risk of subsequent cerebral infarction.

Final Answer: \boxed{D} Antihypertensive therapy

Step 1: Recognize the pancytopenia

- Hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL with MCV 105 μm3 → macrocytic anemia  
- Leukopenia (4,800/mm3) and thrombocytopenia (100,000/mm3) → pancytopenia

B. Limb hypoplasia – seen in thalidomide embryopathy

Step 1: Identify the primary tumor location  
– The lesion is on the apex (tip) of the tongue.

Step 2: Recall lymphatic drainage of the tongue  
– Anterior two-thirds of tongue (including apex) drain first into submental and submandibular lymph nodes.  
– The apex of the tongue drains primarily to the submental lymph nodes (located just beneath the chin).

A. Inferior deep cervical – drains posterior tongue and oropharynx  
B. Parotid – drains lateral face and scalp  
C. Retropharyngeal – drains pharynx, nasal cavity, and palate

Step 1: Recognize acute kidney injury  
– The serum creatinine rises from 0.7 to 1.3 mg/dL in three days, consistent with an acute decrease in glomerular filtration rate.

Step 2: Urinalysis points to intrinsic renal damage  
– “Muddy brown casts” are characteristic of acute tubular necrosis (ATN), an intrinsic renal injury.

Step 3: Identify the pattern of ATN  
– Common causes of ATN include ischemia (e.g., from sepsis and hypotension) and nephrotoxins (e.g., aminoglycosides).  
– Ischemic ATN classically injures the most metabolically active segments of the nephron: the proximal convoluted tubule and the thick ascending limb of Henle’s loop.

Step 4: Choose the single best answer  
– Of the choices given, the proximal convoluted tubule is the most vulnerable site to ischemic injury in ATN.

Final answer: \boxed{Proximal\ tubule}

Step 1: Identify the injured nerve and its location  
- The ulnar nerve at the elbow is part of the peripheral nervous system (PNS).

Step 2: Recall which glial cells myelinate and support axonal regeneration in the PNS  
- In the PNS, Schwann cells both myelinate axons and play a key role in Wallerian degeneration and axonal regrowth.  
- Oligodendrocytes myelinate axons in the central nervous system (CNS) and do not support PNS regeneration.  
- Astrocytes, satellite cells, and tanycytes serve other support roles but do not myelinate peripheral axons or form the scaffolding needed for PNS axonal regrowth.

Step 3: Understand Schwann cell–mediated regeneration  
- After PNS injury, Schwann cells:  
  • Dedifferentiate and proliferate  
  • Phagocytose myelin debris  
  • Secrete neurotrophic factors (e.g., NGF)  
  • Align to form “bands of Büngner” that guide the regrowing axon to its target  

Conclusion: Schwann cells are critical for recovery of neurologic function after a peripheral nerve crush injury.  

Final Answer: \boxed{Schwann\ cells}

Step 1: Interpret the key data  
– 48-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes on metformin + once-daily insulin  
– Hemoglobin A1c = 8.4% (target <7%), so glycemic control is suboptimal  

Step 2: Identify contributing factors  
– Travels frequently, works long hours  
– Meals are usually fast food; sedentary lifestyle  
– Often forgets to check blood glucose  

Step 3: Consider appropriate next steps in management

– Ordering microalbumin is part of complication screening, not directly improving control  
– Measuring blood glucose more often is helpful, but patient already “forgets” to do so—this does not address her main obstacle  
– An exercise regimen or referral to a nutritionist may help, but first one must understand her specific barriers and readiness to change  

Step 4: Apply a patient-centered approach  
– Explore barriers to diet adherence (eg, what prevents her from choosing healthier meals when traveling, how work schedule affects food choices)  
– Understanding these barriers allows the physician to tailor realistic, patient-focused interventions  

Final answer: \boxed{Explore\ barriers\ to\ diet\ adherence}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical picture  
– Two weeks after head trauma the patient develops polyuria and polydipsia  
– Labs show high serum osmolality and low urine osmolality → diabetes insipidus  

Step 2: Differentiate central vs. nephrogenic DI  
– Desmopressin (a vasopressin analog) raises urine osmolality → central DI (deficient ADH release)  

Step 3: Recall the receptor mechanism for vasopressin in the kidney  
– Vasopressin V2 receptors on renal collecting‐duct cells are Gs protein–coupled  
– Gs activation increases adenylyl cyclase activity → ↑ cAMP → insertion of aquaporin‐2 water channels → water reabsorption  

Step 4: Match to the answer choices  
– Activation of adenylyl cyclase is the key signaling step  

Final Answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize acute hypotension and high‐pressure ventilator alarms in an intubated COPD patient  
– Three hours after intubation for COPD exacerbation, the patient develops sudden hypotension (BP 72/46), tachycardia, and ventilator pressure alarms.

Step 2: Differentiate between tension pneumothorax and dynamic hyperinflation (auto‐PEEP)  
– Tension pneumothorax would present with  
 • Unilateral absent breath sounds  
 • Hyperresonance on one side  
 • Tracheal deviation away from the affected side  
– This patient has  
 • Bilaterally decreased breath sounds with wheezing  
 • No tracheal shift  
 • History of severe airflow obstruction (COPD)  
– Jugular venous distention can be seen with both, but the absence of unilateral findings and the presence of expiratory airflow limitation point to auto‐PEEP.

Step 3: Understand auto‐PEEP (dynamic hyperinflation) in COPD  
– In COPD on a ventilator, insufficient expiratory time leads to air trapping and progressive alveolar overdistension (“breath stacking”).  
– Increased intrathoracic pressure reduces venous return and cardiac output, causing hypotension and JVD.

Step 4: Immediate management of auto‐PEEP  
1. Temporarily disconnect the patient from the ventilator to allow trapped gas to escape and assess for hemodynamic improvement.  
2. Adjust ventilator settings to minimize dynamic hyperinflation:  
 • Decrease respiratory rate  
 • Use lower tidal volumes  
 • Increase inspiratory-to-expiratory (I:E) ratio to lengthen expiratory time  
 • Avoid excessive external PEEP in COPD (or set PEEP just above auto‐PEEP if indicated)

Step 5: Eliminate other answer choices  
– B (bilateral chest tubes): not indicated without pneumothoraces  
– C (dopamine): does not address the root cause  
– D (heparin): no evidence of pulmonary embolism  
– E (IV saline bolus): may worsen intrathoracic pressure effects  
– F (needle decompression): for tension pneumothorax, which is not present  

Final Answer:  
\boxed{Adjustment\ of\ the\ ventilator\ settings}

Step 1: Identify the oral lesion  
– White plaques on the lateral tongue that cannot be scraped off point to oral hairy leukoplakia (an EBV‐driven lesion), not candidiasis (which scrapes off).

Step 2: Assess immune status  
– HIV‐positive with CD4+ count of 128/mm3 (<200/mm3), placing him at high risk for AIDS‐defining illnesses and warranting immediate intervention.

Step 3: Consider the options  
A. Antiretroviral therapy – indicated in all patients with HIV, especially those with CD4+ <200  
B. Liquid nitrogen – used for benign lesions like warts, not hairy leukoplakia  
C. Micafungin – an antifungal for candidemia; thrush would scrape off, and this is not a candidal lesion  
D. Surgical excision – not required for hairy leukoplakia  
E. Liposomal doxorubicin – used for Kaposi sarcoma, not indicated here

Step 4: Choose the most appropriate intervention  
– The patient has newly diagnosed AIDS (CD4+ <200) and should be started on combination antiretroviral therapy as soon as possible.

Final Answer: \boxed{Antiretroviral therapy}

Step 1: Recognize the inheritance pattern  
Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive disease. Clinically affected individuals must have bi-allelic (i.e. two) disease-causing CFTR mutations.

Step 2: Interpret the genetic testing  
The panel tested for the 36 most common CFTR mutations. It found only one mutation, G551D, on a single allele.

Step 3: Reconcile one mutation with a recessive disease  
Since CF is recessive, a single mutation cannot explain her full disease phenotype. Either:
  • She has a second CFTR mutation that was missed by the limited panel, or  
  • An alternative explanation (e.g. a somatic “second hit”) accounts for her disease.

Step 4: Rule out distractors  
A. Loss of heterozygosity (“second hit”) is seen in some tumor suppressor disorders (e.g. RB1 in retinoblastoma), not in CFTR-related disease of the pancreas.  
B. CF is not dominant; one G551D allele is insufficient to cause full-blown CF.  
C. An “obligate carrier” is asymptomatic. She is symptomatic.  
D. Her phenotype—pancreatitis, salty sweat, recurrent infections—is classic CF, so the G551D mutation is certainly related.

Step 5: The most parsimonious explanation  
Because the standard panel covers only the most common mutations, many rare CFTR mutations will be missed. Her clinical picture and the one known mutation on one allele imply that she must carry a second, rarer CFTR mutation not included in the panel.

Final Answer: \boxed{E\text{. The second CFTR mutation was not detected by the testing obtained}}

Step 1: Identify the timing and constellation of symptoms  
- Symptoms began ~8 days after starting penicillin.  
- Findings: fever, morbilliform rash, urticaria, edema of hands/feet, arthralgias, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, proteinuria.

Step 2: Recognize the type of hypersensitivity  
- Onset 1–2 weeks after drug exposure, with immune-complex–mediated symptoms (rash, arthralgias, lymphadenopathy, proteinuria) → serum-sickness–like reaction (type III).

Step 3: Recall management of serum-sickness–like reactions to penicillin  
- The reaction is not IgE mediated, so skin testing or antihistamine pretreatment does not prevent it.  
- Recurrence will occur if penicillin is re-administered.  
- The correct preventive strategy is to avoid the offending drug and choose an alternative antibiotic.

Step 4: Apply to this patient  
- To treat future streptococcal pharyngitis in this patient, choose a nonpenicillin antibiotic (e.g., a macrolide).

Final answer:  
\boxed{\text{Use of a nonpenicillin antibiotic to treat the pharyngitis}}

Step 1: Characterize the clinical presentation  
– A 4-year-old asthmatic in the ED acutely decompensates during albuterol treatment, becoming hypotensive (50/20 mm Hg), tachycardic (160/min, thready), gasping, and pale with cold skin.  
– Exam: poor air movement on the left, no wheezes, PMI shifted 2 cm to the left in the 6th intercostal space.  

Step 2: Interpret the chest X-ray findings  
– The radiograph (flattened diaphragms, hyperlucent left hemithorax, mediastinal shift) is classic for a tension pneumothorax on the left.  

Step 3: Identify the type of shock  
– Tension pneumothorax causes obstructive shock by impeding venous return to the right heart, ultimately reducing left ventricular preload and cardiac output.  

Step 4: Match to the answer choices  
A. Albuterol side effects (tachycardia, tremor) do not explain severe hypotension or mediastinal shift.  
B. Methylprednisolone adverse effects (e.g. hyperglycemia, immunosuppression) are not acute causes of shock.  
C. Atelectasis would not cause mediastinal shift away from the lesion or profound hypotension.  
D. Decreased cardiac output – correct, as tension physiology obstructs venous return and drops CO.  
E. Severe bronchospasm alone causes airflow limitation but would still produce wheezes and does not account for mediastinal shift or obstructive shock.  

Final answer: \boxed{Decrease\ in\ cardiac\ output}

Step 1: Clinical presentation

– CT demonstrates a perforated appendix → gross spillage of colonic contents into the peritoneal cavity.

Step 2: Expected peritoneal flora in perforated appendicitis  
– The appendix drains colonic flora.  
– Normal large-bowel flora are mixed anaerobes (e.g., Bacteroides fragilis) and facultative Gram-negative rods (predominantly Escherichia coli).  
– Perforation leads to polymicrobial peritonitis dominated by these organisms.

Step 3: Eliminate the incorrect choices  
A. Candida albicans – opportunistic yeast, not a typical gut pathogen in healthy teens  
B. Citrobacter freundii – uncommon; not a predominant colonic organism  
D. Staphylococcus aureus – skin/soft-tissue pathogen, not gut flora  
E. Streptococcus pneumoniae – respiratory tract pathogen

Step 4: Choose the best answer  
– The most abundant facultative anaerobe in the colon is E. coli.

Final Answer: \boxed{Escherichia\ coli}

Step 1: Clinical presentation

Step 2: Differential considerations  
A. Allergic rhinitis

B. Epstein–Barr virus (infectious mononucleosis)  
  – More prolonged course, significant fatigue, posterior cervical adenopathy, pharyngitis with exudates, hepatosplenomegaly  
C. Mycoplasma pneumoniae  
  – “Atypical” pneumonia: low-grade fever and cough, but usually some pulmonary findings on auscultation and chest x-ray infiltrates  
D. Rhinovirus  
  – Most common cause of the “common cold”: sore throat, nasal discharge, sneezing, headache, low-grade fever, follicular pharyngeal hyperplasia  
E. Streptococcus pyogenes

  The patient’s constellation of mild fever, sore throat, rhinorrhea, cough, headache, and follicular pharyngeal hyperplasia is classic for a viral upper respiratory infection—most commonly rhinovirus.

Step 1: Recognize the graph as a left‐ventricular pressure–volume loop  
- Horizontal axis = LV volume  
- Vertical axis = LV pressure  

The loop proceeds clockwise:  
• Bottom‐right corner = end‐diastolic volume (EDV) just before isovolumetric contraction  
• Top‐right corner = end‐diastolic pressure (after isovolumetric contraction)  
• Top‐left corner = end‐systolic point (after ejection)  
• Bottom‐left corner = end‐systolic pressure (after isovolumetric relaxation)  

Step 2: Identify the labeled points  
(On the exam graph)  
• W = the normal end‐systolic point (top‐left corner of the normal loop)  
• Z = the new end‐systolic point after acute infarct  

Step 3: Determine the immediate effect of acute MI on the loop  
An acute transmural infarct sharply ↓ myocardial contractility ⇒  
  – ↓ slope of the end‐systolic pressure–volume relationship (ESPVR)  
  – ↑ end‐systolic volume (because the ventricle cannot eject as much blood)  
  – ↓ end‐systolic pressure  

Graphically, the top‐left corner of the loop (point W) shifts down and to the right to a new point (point Z).  

Therefore the change in cardiac function in the first 10 seconds after infarction is represented by the shift from W to Z.  

Answer: C. \boxed{W \to Z}

Step 1: Characterize the edema

Step 2: Consider major causes of generalized edema  
- Heart failure: usually has jugular venous distention, crackles, elevated jugular venous pressure, and an elevated BNP.  
- Liver disease (cirrhosis): would have stigmata of chronic liver disease, coagulopathy, abnormal LFTs, and low albumin.  
- Nephrotic syndrome: marked proteinuria leads to hypoalbuminemia and decreased plasma oncotic pressure, causing edema.  

Step 3: Use exam findings to narrow the diagnosis  
- No jugular venous distention, clear lung fields, normal cardiac exam → heart failure is unlikely (would expect ↑BNP and abnormal echocardiogram).

Step 4: Identify the most appropriate diagnostic test  
- Nephrotic syndrome is confirmed by demonstrating significant proteinuria.  

Therefore, the study most likely to be abnormal is the urine protein concentration.  

Final Answer: \boxed{Urine\ protein\ concentration}

Step 1: Identify the core issue  
The patient has frequent asthma exacerbations and relies almost exclusively on the ED for care. He reports that “inhalers don’t work” and feels misunderstood by his doctors, suggesting poor adherence and/or misunderstanding of therapy rather than truly refractory asthma.

Step 2: Recognize the need to explore his perspective  
Before jumping to additional tests, referrals, or blaming allergies, the physician should elicit the patient’s own understanding and beliefs about his medications. This helps uncover misconceptions or concerns that lead to nonadherence.

Step 3: Choose the best open‐ended, nonjudgmental question  
Option C, “Tell me what you know about the steroid inhalers and how they are supposed to work,” invites the patient to share his knowledge and beliefs, giving the physician an opportunity to correct misunderstandings and engage him in a collaborative treatment plan.

Step 1: Clinical presentation  
– A 2-year-old boy with delayed speech (no talking by 15 mo), poor eye contact, hand flapping, irritability → autism spectrum features  
– Macrocephaly (90th % ile), mild growth parameters otherwise  
– Family history of learning difficulties (mother, maternal uncle) → suggests X-linked inheritance  

Step 2: Most likely genetic syndrome  
– Fragile X syndrome is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and an important single‐gene cause of autism spectrum disorder  
– X-linked; carriers often have milder learning problems  

Step 3: Molecular defect in Fragile X  
– Expansion of CGG trinucleotide repeats in the 5′ UTR of the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome  
– When repeats exceed a threshold (>200), the region becomes hypermethylated and FMR1 expression is silenced  

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
A. Alternative splice site → splicing error, not characteristic of Fragile X  
B. Frameshift mutation → small insertions/deletions changing reading frame, not seen in Fragile X  
C. Missense mutation → single amino acid change, not the mechanism here  
D. Nonsense mutation → premature stop codon, again not the mechanism  
E. Trinucleotide repeat expansion → exactly the mechanism of Fragile X  

Final answer: \boxed{Trinucleotide\;repeat\;expansion}

Step 1: Clinical presentation

– Symptoms worsen with emotional stress  
– Family history (maternal grandmother and mother) with similar late‐onset movement disorder in an autosomal‐dominant pattern  

Step 2: Neurologic findings  
– Normal strength, bulk, tone (rules out primary motor neuron or muscle disease)  
– No cerebellar signs (no intention tremor or dysmetria)  
– Intact sensation (rules out dorsal columns)  
– Impaired gaze fixation and tongue protrusion (ocular pursuit abnormalities are seen in Huntington disease)  
– Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes (early upper motor neuron signs in Huntington disease)  
– Normal mental status now (cognitive decline comes later in Huntington disease)  

Step 3: Most likely diagnosis  
– Early Huntington disease presenting with chorea, ocular pursuit defects, hyperreflexia, positive family history  

Step 4: Pathophysiology of Huntington disease  
– Selective degeneration of medium spiny GABAergic neurons in the caudate nucleus (a component of the basal ganglia)  
– Results in decreased inhibition of thalamocortical circuits and choreiform movements  

Final Answer  
The anatomic abnormality is located in the basal ganglia.

- Platelet count is 15,000/mm³ (normal 150,000–400,000/mm³) → marked thrombocytopenia

Step 2: Correlate thrombocytopenia with bleeding manifestations  
- Platelets are critical for primary hemostasis (formation of the platelet plug)  
- Severe thrombocytopenia (< 20,000/mm³) leads to petechiae, mucosal bleeding (eg, epistaxis, gum bleeding)  
- Deep hemorrhages (eg, hemarthroses, visceral hematomas) are more characteristic of coagulation factor deficiencies  

Step 3: Match the expected physical finding  
- Petechiae are pinpoint nonblanching hemorrhages in the skin or mucous membranes caused by platelet deficiency  
- Other choices (deep venous thrombosis, hemarthrosis, visceral hematoma) are not typical of isolated thrombocytopenia

- Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) = 100 × 0.05 = 5/mm³ → severe neutropenia.

Step 2: Recall appropriate pharmacotherapy  
- In chemotherapy‐induced neutropenia, the treatment of choice to stimulate neutrophil recovery is granulocyte colony‐stimulating factor (G‐CSF), i.e. filgrastim.

Step 3: Eliminate distractors

- Dexamethasone → corticosteroid  
- Interferon α → antiviral/immunomodulator  
- Interleukin‐2 → T‐cell growth factor  
- Leucovorin → folinic acid rescue after methotrexate

Step 1: Identify the coagulation defect  
– The patient is a young man with excessive bleeding, a family history of “bleeders” on the maternal side, a normal platelet count, bleeding time, and PT, but a prolonged PTT.  
– This clinical picture and inheritance pattern point to hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency), an X-linked recessive disorder.

Step 2: Recall X-linked recessive inheritance  
– Affected males (X^hY) cannot transmit the disease allele to their sons (they pass on the Y chromosome) but will transmit their single mutant X chromosome to all daughters.  
– Daughters of an affected father thus become obligate carriers (X^hX), usually without clinical bleeding.  
– A carrier daughter has a 50% chance of passing the mutant X to each child:  
 • Sons who inherit X^h will be affected (X^hY)  
 • Daughters who inherit X^h will be carriers (X^hX)

Step 3: Apply to this patient’s offspring  
– His sons: receive his Y chromosome → cannot be affected.  
– His daughters: receive his mutant X → will be carriers, but generally asymptomatic.  
– Those carrier daughters, when they have children, each son has a 50% chance of being affected.

Therefore, the most accurate statement regarding risk to future generations is:  
“The sons of their daughters will be at risk for a clinically evident coagulation disorder.”

Answer: \boxed{C}

Step 2: Induction of P450 increases the metabolic clearance of various substrates, including vitamin D (accelerating its conversion to inactive metabolites).  
Step 3: Faster metabolism of vitamin D lowers its circulating levels, contributing to decreased calcium absorption and potential bone loss.  
Therefore, carbamazepine most likely affects the metabolism of vitamin D.

Step 1: Recognize the exposure and timing  
– A farmer who spills an agricultural (organophosphate) insecticide on his clothes  
– Symptoms begin within minutes: blurred vision, vomiting, diarrhea, bronchospasm, muscle weakness

Step 2: Identify the cholinergic crisis  
– Organophosphates irreversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase → ↑ACh at synapses  
– Muscarinic effects (“DUMBBELSS”): Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis (blurred vision), Bronchospasm, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Sweating, Salivation  
– Nicotinic effects: muscle weakness, fatigue  
– CNS effects possible (confusion, seizures)

Step 3: Evaluate vital signs and complications  
– Bradycardia (30/min), hypotension (90/60 mm Hg)  
– Cheyne–Stokes respirations, wheezing/gurgling → respiratory failure risk  
– Dehydration, hypoxemia, hypercapnia

Step 4: Choose the antidote  
– Immediate goal: reverse life-threatening muscarinic effects (bronchospasm, bradycardia, secretions)  
– Atropine: competitive muscarinic antagonist → dries secretions, opens airways, raises heart rate  
– Pralidoxime (2-PAM) is given to reactivate acetylcholinesterase (especially for nicotinic/muscle effects), but atropine is the first immediate step

Answer: The most appropriate immediate pharmacotherapy is atropine.  

Final answer: \boxed{A\text{. Atropine}}

Step 3: Physical exam

– In pericarditis, the serous pericardium (visceral and parietal layers) is inflamed and injured

Step 1: Assess the pH  
– pH = 7.30 (< 7.35) → acidemia  

Step 2: Determine which component is primary  
– pCO₂ = 28 mm Hg (low) would cause alkalosis, not acidosis → respiratory disorder is compensatory, not primary  
– HCO₃⁻ = 14 mEq/L (low) would cause acidosis → primary disorder is metabolic acidosis  

Step 3: Check for appropriate respiratory compensation  
– Winter’s formula for expected pCO₂ in metabolic acidosis:  
  expected pCO₂ ≈ 1.5 × [HCO₃⁻] + 8 ±2  
  = 1.5 × 14 + 8 ±2  
  = 21 + 8 ±2  
  = 29 ±2 mm Hg  
– Measured pCO₂ is 28 mm Hg, which is within the expected range → appropriate respiratory compensation  

Final Answer: \boxed{Metabolic\ acidosis,\ respiratory\ compensation}

Step 1: Identify the lesion  
– The patient has open and closed comedones (blackheads and whiteheads), characteristic of acne vulgaris.

Step 2: Recall the pathogenesis of acne vulgaris  
1. Follicular hyperkeratinization → plug (microcomedo) formation  
2. Increased sebum production by sebaceous glands  
3. Colonization by Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes)  
4. Inflammation

Step 3: Determine what drives increased sebum production  
– Sebaceous glands are highly sensitive to androgens (particularly dihydrotestosterone).  
– Androgens increase the size and activity of sebaceous glands, leading to excess sebum, follicular plugging, and acne.

Step 4: Evaluate the answer choices  
A. Decreased parasympathetic stimulation – no, parasympathetic input does not drive sebum production.  
B. Increased estrogen stimulation – estrogens actually tend to decrease sebum production.  
C. Increased responsiveness to FSH – FSH acts on ovarian follicles, not sebaceous glands.  
D. Increased sympathetic stimulation – sympathetic nervous system is not the primary regulator of sebaceous gland activity.  
E. Stimulation of the sebaceous glands by androgens – correct, androgens (eg, DHT) ↑ sebum → acne.

Final answer: \boxed{Stimulation\ of\ the\ sebaceous\ glands\ by\ androgens}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical syndrome

– Thyroid hormones (T3 and T4) are elevated because the thyroid gland is overactive.

Step 1. Identify the clinical syndrome

– β-lactams (amoxicillin, cefaclor) target cell-wall organisms and are ineffective against Mycoplasma (no cell wall).

– Fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) have some activity but are not first-line in young, healthy outpatients.

Step 1: Identify the key neurologic finding  
– The patient has a “postural and action tremor of the forearms and hands accompanied by a sudden, brief loss of muscle strength,” which is a description of asterixis (“flapping tremor”).  

Step 2: Recognize the underlying disorder  
– Asterixis plus lethargy in a patient with alcoholic cirrhosis and elevated bilirubin strongly suggests hepatic encephalopathy, caused by accumulation of neurotoxic ammonia. Infection is unlikely (negative cultures), Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency) presents with ataxia and ophthalmoplegia, and benzodiazepines (lorazepam) would worsen encephalopathy.  

Step 3: Choose the appropriate treatment  
– Lactulose is metabolized by gut bacteria into acids that convert ammonia (NH3) to ammonium (NH4+), which cannot be reabsorbed, thereby lowering serum ammonia levels and improving both cognition and asterixis.  

Final Answer:  
\boxed{\text{Oral lactulose therapy (Choice C)}}

Step 1: Identify the key elements of a properly written prescription  
  1. Use a leading zero for decimal doses (e.g., “0.5%,” not “.5%”).  
  2. Do not use a trailing zero (e.g., “1,” not “1.0”).  
  3. Spell out the route and frequency in plain English rather than using Latin abbreviations (e.g., “right eye” instead of “OD,” “four times daily” instead of “QID”).  

Step 2: Evaluate the answer choices  
  A. Ketorolac 0.5% solution, 1 drop in right eye, four times daily  
     – Meets all guidelines: leading zero, no trailing zero, plain‐English route (“right eye”) and frequency (“four times daily”).  
  B. Ketorolac .5% solution, 1 drop in right eye QID  
     – Missing leading zero; uses “QID.”  
  C. Ketorolac 0.5% solution, 1 drop OD four times daily  
     – Uses “OD” instead of “right eye.”  
  D. Ketorolac 0.5% solution, 1 drop OD, QID  
     – Uses both “OD” and “QID.”  

Step 3: Select the most appropriate prescription  
  Choice A is the only option that adheres to all safety and clarity standards for prescribing.

Final Answer:
\boxed{\text{Ketorolac 0.5% solution, 1 drop in right eye, four times daily}}

• First, determine thyroid function (TSH) → already done and normal  
• Next, obtain imaging to characterize the nodule and guide further management

• Ultrasonography is the preferred initial imaging modality for thyroid nodules

Step 1: Summarize key findings  
• 36-week male infant, born by cesarean § no labor, meconium-stained fluid

A. Chlamydial pneumonia – presents 3–19 weeks of age with subacute pneumonia; not at 2 h.  
B. Group B streptococcal sepsis – causes significant respiratory distress, often within hours, with systemic signs; CXR usually shows diffuse pneumonia or ARDS pattern.

E. Transient tachypnea of the newborn (TTN) – risk factors: late preterm (34–37 weeks), elective cesarean (no labor), meconium-stained fluid; onset in first hours; mild distress; CXR shows perihilar interstitial streaking and fluid in fissures.  

Step 3: Match findings to diagnosis  
This infant’s mild tachypnea, good oxygenation, cesarean delivery without labor, and classic CXR appearance of fluid in the fissures point to TTN.  

Final Answer
